content.text:
Notation:
• The statement that \((X,\mathcal{B},\mu,f)\) is a mpt (measure preserving transformation) means that \(X\) is a set with the probability measure \(\mu\) defined on the \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{B}\) and \(f\) is a bijective, bi-measurable map \(f:X\to X\) which preserves the measure \(\mu\).
• The statement that \((X,d,\mathcal{B},\mu,f)\) is a mph (measure preserving homeomorphism) means that \((X,\mathcal{B},\mu,f)\) is a mpt and in addition, \((X,d)\) is a compact metric space, \(f\) is a homeomorphism, and \(\mathcal{B}\) is the Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra.

body.text: Let \((X,d)\) be a compact metric space and \(f:X\to X\) a homeomorphism.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define the recurrent set \(\Lambda(f)\) and the nonwandering set \(\Omega(f)\) and show they are both compact, invariant sets.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show that \(\Lambda(f)\subset\Omega(f)\) and give an example where the inclusion is proper.

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Define the omega-limit set \(\omega(x,f)\) of a point \(x\in X\) and show it is a compact, invariant set.

subparts.label: (d)
subparts.text: Prove or disprove: For every \(x\in X\), that \(\omega(x,f)\subset\Lambda(f)\).

body.text: Let \((X,d)\) be a compact metric space and \(f:X\to X\) a homeomorphism.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Show that there is always an \(f\)-invariant subset \(Z\subset X\) so that \(f\) restricted to \(Z\) is minimal.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show that there is always a measure \(\mu\) so that \((X,d,\mathcal{B},\mu,f)\) is a mph.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define the support, \(\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\), of the measure \(\mu\) and show it is a compact, invariant set.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show that almost every point in \(\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\) is recurrent.

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Show that every point in \(\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\) is nonwandering.

subparts.label: (d)
subparts.text: Show that \(\mu(\Omega(f))=1\).

subparts.label: (e)
subparts.text: If \((X,d,\mathcal{B},\mu,f)\) is ergodic, show that \(f\) is full orbit transitive on \(\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\).

subparts.label: (f)
subparts.text: Show that \(\operatorname{supp}(\mu)\subset\Lambda(f)\), where \(\Lambda(f)\) is the recurrent set of \(f\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Birkhoff’s pointwise ergodic theorem

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: von Neumann’s \(L^2\)-mean ergodic theorem

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define the one-sided subshift of finite type \(\Sigma_A^+\) determined by \(A\) and the shift map \(\sigma:\Sigma_A^+\to\Sigma_A^+\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Define one of the standard metrics on \(\Sigma_A^+\).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Show that periodic points of \(\sigma\) are dense in \(\Sigma_A^+\).

subparts.label: (d)
subparts.text: Show that \((\Sigma_A^+,\sigma)\) is forward orbit transitive.

subparts.label: (e)
subparts.text:
Let \(N_k\) be the number of fixed points of \(\sigma^k\) in \(\Sigma_A^+\). Compute explicitly
\[
\lim_{k\to\infty}\frac{\log(N_k)}{k}
\]
and justify your answer completely.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Define the Bernoulli measure \(\mu_p\) on the two-sided shift on two symbols \(\Sigma_2\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Show that \((\Sigma_2,\mathcal{B},\sigma,\mu_p)\) is strong mixing (you don’t have to prove it is a mpt, you may assume that is true).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text:
Define \(\alpha:\Sigma_2\to\mathbb{R}\) by
\[
\alpha(\ldots s_{-1}s_0s_1s_2\ldots)=2s_{-1}-s_0+s_2^2.
\]
Compute the following limit for \(\mu_p\)-almost every sequence \(\underline{s}\in\Sigma_2\):
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}\alpha(\sigma^i(\underline{s})).
\]
and justify your answer completely.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Show that the origin is an unstable fixed point for \(f\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Find an invariant, Borel probability measure for \(f\) (Hint: don’t try too hard).

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Show that your invariant measure in (b) is the only invariant, Borel probability measure.
